critic_raw:
This design forecasts monthly retail revenue using an XGBoost+LSTM ensemble versus ARIMA, evaluated on a 12-point test set.

The test set contains 12 monthly observations. MAPE on 12 data points cannot produce a statistically interpretable comparison between two forecasting methods. With 12 test samples, the variance of MAPE is enormous: a single outlier month (e.g., one extreme holiday season) can swing MAPE by tens of percentage points. The design has no mechanism to distinguish genuine systematic superiority from random variation over these 12 points. No statistical test is proposed for the primary metric comparison — the Ljung-Box test checks residual structure, not comparison significance. This is a fundamental statistical power problem that renders the test evaluation inconclusive regardless of the direction of results.

The ARIMA baseline is constrained to univariate revenue only; no external regressors (holiday months, seasonal indicators) are added. The ensemble's XGBoost component, by contrast, uses month-of-year one-hot indicators and a binary holiday flag as features. This means the ensemble has access to calendar structure that the ARIMA model is explicitly denied. ARIMAX (ARIMA with external regressors) is the appropriate comparison for a fair test, not univariate ARIMA. The design as written tests 'structured + holiday features vs. no structured features,' not 'ensemble architecture vs. ARIMA architecture.'

Detrending is performed by fitting a linear trend on the training set (months 1–36) and subtracting it from all data. For a 60-month revenue series, a single linear trend applied across all 60 months imposes a global stationarity assumption. If the trend has a structural break (common in retail due to expansions, closures, or the 2020 disruption period embedded in a 5-year window), the detrended series will have systematic non-stationarity that both models must absorb — potentially favoring the ARIMA's built-in differencing over the ensemble's global trend-removal approach.
all_issues_raised: The test set contains 12 monthly observations — this sample size provides insufficient statistical power to distinguish genuine model superiority from random variation in MAPE, and no significance test is proposed for the primary comparison.

The ARIMA baseline is restricted to univariate revenue with no external regressors, while the ensemble's XGBoost component uses holiday indicators and month-of-year features — this is not a fair architectural comparison.

Linear detrending using a global trend fitted on months 1–36 imposes a single-slope stationarity assumption across 60 months; structural breaks in the revenue series will produce systematic residual non-stationarity that affects both models unequally.
all_issues_adjudicated: The test set contains 12 monthly observations — this sample size provides insufficient statistical power to distinguish genuine model superiority from random variation in MAPE, and no significance test is proposed for the primary comparison.

The ARIMA baseline is restricted to univariate revenue with no external regressors, while the ensemble's XGBoost component uses holiday indicators and month-of-year features — this is not a fair architectural comparison.

Linear detrending using a global trend fitted on months 1–36 imposes a single-slope stationarity assumption across 60 months; structural breaks in the revenue series will produce systematic residual non-stationarity that affects both models unequally.
